caption: Figure 1. (a) Prior works learn each instrument prompt in- dependently. (b,c) They overlook the power of positive for- ward and backward transfer between early-acquired and newly- acquired knowledge. (d) In contrast, our work organizes instru- ment prompts from general to specific as a hierarchical prompt parsing tree. (e) It supports positive forward transfer by helping new instrument prompts inherit profitable knowledge from the past to assist new-class learning. (f) It further enables positive back- ward transfer by letting the newly-acquired knowledge wisely re- fine early-acquired knowledge to improve old-class segmentation.
description2: A hierarchical prompt parsing tree is plotted, structured with instrument prompts organized from general to specific.
description3: Two-panel diagrams show prior work's independent learning versus a hierarchical prompt parsing tree enabling positive forward and backward transfer.
questions: How do early-acquired and newly-acquired knowledge interact in the presented approach?
What is the hierarchical structure used to organize instrument prompts?

caption: Figure 2. Overview of our self-reflection hierarchical prompt framework. We progressively append instrument-aware prompts into a pre- trained model during the training lifespan. For each new class, before diving into learning its specific prompts, we first build a hierarchical prompt parsing tree to discover the profitable knowledge it could inherit from the past, i.e., the instrument-shared (IS) and n-part-shared knowledge, promoting new-class learning by positive forward knowledge transfer. Simply learning one instrument-distinct (ID) prompt partition would be sufficient to delineate its contour. Based on the newly acquired ID partition, we trigger self-reflection on existing knowledge to support positive backward knowledge transfer, wisely refining their representativeness within the context of all seen classes without causing catastrophic forgetting.
description2: Our self-reflection hierarchical prompt framework progressively appends instrument-aware prompts into a pre-trained model during training for profitable knowledge discovery and transfer.
description3: A hierarchical prompt framework diagram displays prompt partitions, trees, and knowledge transfer pathways between a model encoder and prediction head.
questions: How does the framework leverage past knowledge to improve learning of new classes?
What is the overall structure used to organize and refine prompts during training?

caption: Figure 3. Visual comparisons of our approaches and highly competitive approaches. More comparison results are in the supplementary.
description2: A visual comparison is plotted between our approaches and highly competitive approaches.
description3: A multi-panel plot displays visual comparisons of several approaches alongside ground truth, arranged in horizontal rows and columns.
questions: How do different approaches perform relative to each other in these visual comparisons?
What is the overall trend shown by the comparison results presented here?

caption: Figure 4. Effects of the decay factor γ and the teleport probability α on model performance.
description2: The figure plots the effects of the decay factor gamma and the teleport probability alpha on model performance.
description3: Two-panel line charts display performance (mIoU) versus decay factor (γ) and teleport probability (α), respectively, with labeled axes and data points.
questions: How does the decay factor influence the model's overall performance?
What is the general relationship between teleport probability and model performance?

caption: Figure 2. Visualization comparison of segmentation results across all methods. Rows 1–2, 4-5: previous datasets. Row 3, 6: current dataset.
description2: The figure visualizes a comparison of segmentation results across various methods, with rows 1-2 and 4-5 representing previous datasets and row 3 and 6 representing the current dataset.
description3: Two-panel image displays a grid of segmentation results across multiple methods for various datasets, with rows representing different datasets.
questions: What is the purpose of the comparison shown between methods and datasets?
How are the results organized to show differences between datasets?

caption: Figure 3. Automatic estimation of instrument part count.
description2: The figure presents an automatic estimation of instrument part count.
description3: A flowchart details an automatic instrument part count estimation process, starting with labeled images and ending with a three-part instrument result.
questions: What is the overall process for estimating the number of parts in an instrument?
How is the count of instrument parts determined automatically?
